--

Why your next home server shouldn’t be a Raspberry Pi, and how to build a battery-backed Proxmox cluster for zero dollars.

Part 1: Turning an Old Dell into a Proxmox Powerhouse
When it comes to building a home server, the hardware options can feel endless. You could easily go out and buy a Raspberry Pi, a Zimaboard, or a dedicated mini-PC. But since this is a budget build, we are going to use something you likely already have lying around: an old laptop.
For this build, I am using a Dell Inspiron powered by an Intel i3 processor, 8GB of RAM, and a 512GB SSD.
While a laptop might seem unconventional for a server, it comes with one massive, built-in advantage: the battery. In the event of a power outage, your server doesn't instantly crash and risk data corruption. The laptop battery acts as a free Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS), giving you built-in backup power right out of the box.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Proxmox cluster usage in GrafanaChoosing the Foundation: Operating Systems & Hardware Constraints
Once the hardware is sorted, the next hurdle is the operating system. You generally have two paths: Windows or Linux.
Windows is a heavy OS designed primarily for its Graphical User Interface (GUI). Since we will be hosting applications over the network and managing them remotely, we don't need a heavy desktop interface eating up our valuable CPU and RAM. Because efficiency is the goal, Linux is the clear winner.
However, Linux isn't just one operating system; it comes in various flavors like Debian, Ubuntu, and Arch Linux. For our server, we will be using a specialized virtualization OS: Proxmox Virtual Environment (VE).
Choosing Proxmox is a game-changer, but it dictates two hard technical rules about our setup:

• The x86_64 Architecture Limit: Official Proxmox VE only supports 64-bit Intel and AMD processors. While there are unofficial community ports for ARM devices, running the official x86 version guarantees maximum stability and compatibility with the widest range of container images. This is exactly why an old Dell laptop with an Intel i3 works beautifully, while a Raspberry Pi does not.

• The Ethernet Requirement: While Proxmox is Debian under the hood and can technically be forced to use Wi-Fi, for all practical purposes, treat Proxmox as Ethernet-only. The OS expects a hardwired connection to create the default network bridge (“vmbr0”). This bridge is how your future VMs and containers will share the host's internet connection. Bridging a wireless adapter is notoriously unstable, drops packets, and often requires complicated routing (NAT). Save yourself the headache and plan to plug the laptop directly into your router.
Why Proxmox VE is the Undisputed King of the Home Server
If you’re still running your self-hosted apps directly on a messy bare-metal OS, it’s time to pivot. Proxmox VE provides a web-based virtualization platform that lets you spin up different Virtual Machines (VMs) and containers, giving you a personal cloud experience similar to AWS or GCP.
Eventually, this server will be the central hub for everything from logging data off hand-wired radio circuits to running dedicated Python machine learning models. To do that cleanly, we need isolation. Here is why Proxmox is the perfect foundation:

• Bare-Metal Performance: As a Type-1 hypervisor built on Debian Linux, Proxmox installs directly onto your hardware. You get near-native performance without the overhead of a bloated desktop OS.

• The Best of Both Worlds: Proxmox unifies heavy-duty VMs and ultra-lightweight Linux Containers (LXC) under a single, clean web UI.

• Rock-Solid Storage (Native ZFS): Proxmox features native support for ZFS, giving you enterprise-grade data protection, bit-rot prevention, and lightning-fast software-defined storage right out of the box.

• Built-in Backups & Snapshots: Tinkering means breaking things. Proxmox lets you take instant snapshots before a risky update. If a container goes sideways, you can roll back to a working state in seconds.

• Truly Free and Open Source: You get enterprise-grade clustering, high availability, and network management for exactly zero dollars.

Step 1: Prepping the Dell for its New Life
Before we install anything, we need to get the hardware ready.

• Download the Proxmox VE 9.2 ISO: directly from the official Proxmox Downloads portal (https://www.proxmox.com/en/downloads).

• Flash it to a USB drive: (8GB or larger) using a free flashing utility like BalenaEtcher (https://etcher.balena.io/) or Rufus (https://rufus.ie/).

• Plug in an Ethernet cable: As discussed, we need a hardwired connection to ensure the network bridge is created successfully during setup.
Next, boot up the Dell and hammer the ‘F2’ or ‘F12’ key to enter the BIOS. You need to change two critical settings before installing:

• Disable Secure Boot: Proxmox is built on Debian Linux, and Secure Boot will often block the installation.

• Enable Virtualization (Intel VT-x): If this is disabled, Proxmox will install, but it will throw errors when you try to actually spin up a VM later.
Warning: Installing Proxmox will completely wipe all existing data on the laptop’s drive. Back up anything important before proceeding.
Step 2: The Installation Wizard
Plug in your freshly flashed USB drive, boot from it, and select Install Proxmox VE.
The graphical installer is straightforward, but pay attention to these two critical screens:

• The Hard Disk Selection:
Select your 512GB SSD. Click the “Options” button next to it to choose your filesystem. Since we are working with an i3 processor and 8GB of RAM, select ext4. While ZFS is an incredible file system, it is memory-hungry. Ext4 is lightweight and perfect for a single-drive laptop setup.

• Location and Time Zone:
The installer will usually auto-detect your location and time zone based on your network connection. This location helps Proxmox select the fastest nearby download servers for updates. Confirm your country and keyboard layout are correct.

• Credentials (Password and Email):
Next, set the password for the superuser (“root”). This is the master key to your server, so make it strong — aim for at least 12 characters mixing uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols.
You must also enter a valid email address. The system uses this to send critical alerts to the administrator, such as notifications for available package updates or error messages from scheduled background tasks (cron jobs).

• The Network Configuration:
This is a critical step. First, ensure your management network interface is selected (look for the filled circle indicating the port is physically “UP”).
* Hostname: Enter a recognizable domain name (e.g. “proxmox.local”).
* IP Address: Do not leave this on DHCP. Assign a Static IPv4 Address outside your router’s normal assignment range (for example, “192.168.1.100”).
Note: The installer allows either IPv4 or IPv6, but not both simultaneously at this stage. (If you need a dual-stack setup, you can add the second IP later).

• Summary and Installation:
The final screen displays a summary of your selected options. Carefully re-check your static IP and target disk. Use the Previous button if anything looks wrong.
Once you click Install, the installer will format the SSD and copy the necessary packages. Depending on your USB drive and SSD speeds, this takes a few minutes. When the setup finishes, the system will automatically reboot. Be sure to remove your USB installation drive so the laptop boots from its internal SSD.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Configuring target disk and selecting ext4 filesystem (​Image source: Proxmox Documentation)Press enter or click to view image in full size

Configuring network (​Image source: Proxmox Documentation)Step 3: The “Lid Close” Trick (Laptop-to-Server Conversion)
When the Dell reboots, you will be greeted by a stark black screen with a login prompt and a web address (e.g. “https://192.168.1.100:8006”).
Your hypervisor is officially running. You can now step away from the Dell.
Fire up the browser on your daily driver and navigate to that IP address. (Your browser might throw a security warning because the server uses a self-signed SSL certificate — just click “Advanced” and proceed). You can log in to the web management console using the username “root” and the strong password you just created.
You can manage the entire server directly from this web UI, but because we are using a laptop, there is one major hardware problem we have to solve first: sleep mode.
By default, Linux is designed to suspend the system when the laptop lid is closed. If you close your Dell right now and slide it onto a shelf, your entire home server will go to sleep. To fix this, we need to tell the operating system to ignore the lid switch.
Open the terminal on your daily driver and log into your new server via SSH:
ssh root@192.168.1.100Open the login daemon configuration file using the nano text editor:
nano /etc/systemd/logind.confScroll down through the file until you find a line that looks like this:
“#HandleLidSwitch=suspend”
You need to uncomment that line (remove the `#`) and change “suspend” to “ignore”. It should look exactly like this:
HandleLidSwitch=ignoreSave the file by pressing `Ctrl + O`, hit `Enter`, and then exit with `Ctrl + X`.
Finally, restart the service to apply the changes:
systemctl restart systemd-logindYou can now safely shut the lid on your Dell laptop. The screen will turn off to save power, but the Proxmox server will keep humming along in the background. You’ve successfully transformed an old laptop into a headless, battery-backed virtualization powerhouse.

Up Next: Right now, our Proxmox server is empty. To make it useful, we need a place to store our files, host our media, and back up our data. In Part 2, we are going to carve out a chunk of our server to build a robust Network Attached Storage (NAS) using OpenMediaVault (OMV).
If you found this setup guide helpful, please drop a clap (or 50!) and follow me so you don’t miss Part 2, where we will build the NAS backend.
